examples.title: Liang-Barsky Line Clipping Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of parametric line clipping against a rectangular window
examples.code:
// Liang-Barsky Line Clipping Algorithm
// Input: Line from (x1,y1) to (x2,y2), Clipping window (xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax)
// Output: Clipped line coordinates or indication if line is completely outside

PROCEDURE LiangBarsky(x1, y1, x2, y2, xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax):
 // Calculate direction differences
 dx = x2 - x1
 dy = y2 - y1
 
 // Initialize parameter values
 u1 = 0.0 // Entering parameter
 u2 = 1.0 // Exiting parameter
 
 // Define p and q arrays for 4 edges
 p[1] = -dx // Left edge
 q[1] = x1 - xmin
 p[2] = dx // Right edge
 q[2] = xmax - x1
 p[3] = -dy // Bottom edge
 q[3] = y1 - ymin
 p[4] = dy // Top edge
 q[4] = ymax - y1
 
 // Process each clipping edge
 FOR i = 1 TO 4:
 IF p[i] = 0 THEN:
 // Line is parallel to this edge
 IF q[i] < 0 THEN:
 RETURN "Line completely outside" // Reject
 END IF
 ELSE:
 // Calculate parameter value
 r = q[i] / p[i]
 
 IF p[i] < 0 THEN:
 // Entering edge - update u1
 IF r > u1 THEN:
 u1 = r
 END IF
 ELSE:
 // Exiting edge - update u2
 IF r < u2 THEN:
 u2 = r
 END IF
 END IF
 END IF
 
 // Early rejection test
 IF u1 > u2 THEN:
 RETURN "Line completely outside"
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Calculate clipped endpoints
 x1_clip = x1 + u1 * dx
 y1_clip = y1 + u1 * dy
 x2_clip = x1 + u2 * dx
 y2_clip = y1 + u2 * dy
 
 RETURN (x1_clip, y1_clip, x2_clip, y2_clip)
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: The algorithm processes each clipping edge sequentially. For each edge, it checks if the line is parallel (p=0) and outside (q<0), then rejects if so. Otherwise, it updates the entering parameter u1 for entering edges (p<0) and exiting parameter u2 for exiting edges (p>0). If u1 ≤ u2 after all edges, the line is clipped using the computed parameters.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - uses fixed number of variables

examples.title: Python Implementation of Liang-Barsky
examples.description: Complete working implementation with visualization support
examples.code:
def liang_barsky_clip(x1, y1, x2, y2, xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax):
 '''
 Liang-Barsky Line Clipping Algorithm
 Returns clipped coordinates or None if line is completely outside
 '''
 # Direction coefficients
 dx = x2 - x1
 dy = y2 - y1
 
 # Initialize parameters
 u1, u2 = 0.0, 1.0
 
 # Define p and q for each edge
 # Edge 0: Left (x = xmin)
 p = [-dx, dx, -dy, dy]
 q = [x1 - xmin, xmax - x1, y1 - ymin, ymax - y1]
 
 # Process each clipping edge
 for i in range(4):
 if p[i] == 0:
 # Line parallel to edge
 if q[i] < 0:
 return None # Line is outside
 else:
 r = q[i] / p[i]
 
 if p[i] < 0:
 # Entering edge
 u1 = max(u1, r)
 else:
 # Exiting edge
 u2 = min(u2, r)
 
 # Early exit if parameters cross
 if u1 > u2:
 return None
 
 # Calculate clipped endpoints
 x1_clipped = x1 + u1 * dx
 y1_clipped = y1 + u1 * dy
 x2_clipped = x1 + u2 * dx
 y2_clipped = y1 + u2 * dy
 
 return (x1_clipped, y1_clipped, x2_clipped, y2_clipped)

# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Clip line from (50, 100) to (200, 150) against window (100, 100, 180, 180)
 result = liang_barsky_clip(50, 100, 200, 150, 100, 100, 180, 180)
 if result:
 print(f"Clipped line: ({result[0]:.2f}, {result[1]:.2f}) to ({result[2]:.2f}, {result[3]:.2f})")
 else:
 print("Line completely outside clipping window")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the complete Liang-Barsky algorithm with proper handling of parallel lines, early rejection, and parameter updates. The function returns None for completely outside lines and the clipped coordinates otherwise.

examples.title: Step-by-Step Example Execution
examples.description: Walkthrough of Liang-Barsky with specific numerical example
examples.explanation: This step-by-step walkthrough shows how the algorithm processes each edge, updates parameters, and computes final clipped coordinates. The line enters through the left edge (u1=0.33) and exits through the right edge (u2=0.87).

examples.title: Comparison: Cohen-Sutherland vs Liang-Barsky
examples.description: Comparison of computational steps between both algorithms
examples.explanation: This comparison shows why Liang-Barsky is more efficient: it performs all computations in a single pass through the four edges, while Cohen-Sutherland may require multiple iterations with endpoint re-computation for partially clipped lines.
examples.timeComplexity: Cohen-Sutherland: O(1) but with more comparisons; Liang-Barsky: O(1) with fewer comparisons
